---
title: "Welcome to the Paddling Pool"
---

> **paddling pool** /ˈpadlɪŋ ˌpuːl/ · *noun*
>
> A shallow body of water in which one can comfortably wade rather than swim — a place to get the feel of the water before going further.

This site is the statistical equivalent: a low-pressure refresher in the analyses you'll need for your thesis or specialist master's courses. Work through topics at your own pace, watch short JASP demonstrations, and practise with real datasets — a shallow place to revisit the essentials before going further with your own research.

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## What this site builds on
Most of the content here is repackaged from existing teaching material: the [JASP video library](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/) and [how-to-use-JASP](https://jasp-stats.org/how-to-use-jasp/) blog posts, the textbook [*Discovering Statistics Using JASP*](https://discoverjasp.com/), and the UvA [*Scientific and Statistical Reasoning*](https://studiegids.uva.nl/en/courses/2025/1/54082) course lectures. What's added on top is the structure — short, decision-shaped pages with consistent layout and cross-references between them.
:::

## How to use this site

1. **Start with [Getting Started](getting-started.qmd)** if you have not used JASP before, or need a quick refresh on loading data and navigating the interface.
2. **Work through [Basics](core/descriptives.qmd)** — descriptive statistics, t-tests, ANOVA, regression, non-parametric tests, and contingency tables. These are the foundations that almost every analysis builds on.
3. **Consult [Advanced Topics](advanced/mediation-moderation.qmd)** when your thesis or course requires a specific technique. Not everyone needs all of these — pick what is relevant to you.

Each topic page follows the same structure:

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## What you will find on each topic page
- **What is it?** — a short conceptual overview
- **When to use it** — decision guide: which research question, which data type
- **Assumptions** — what to check before interpreting your results
- **How to do it in JASP** — menu paths and links to short screen recordings in the JASP Video Library
- **Interpreting the output** — what to report and how to read the numbers
- **Further reading** — textbook chapters (DSUJ, Agresti, plus discipline-specific references on the advanced pages) and blog posts
- **Further viewing** — links to the full SSR lecture recording for the topic, where applicable
- **Try it yourself** — concrete exercises with a linked JASP dataset and a worked solution
:::

---

## Basics

These topics cover the analyses expected of every master's student.

::: {.topic-grid}
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Descriptive Statistics](core/descriptives.qmd)
Summarise and visualise your data before running any inferential test. Mean, SD, histograms, boxplots, raincloud plots.
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [t-Tests](core/t-tests.qmd)
Compare one or two means. One-sample, paired-samples, and independent-samples t-tests — with effect sizes and assumption checks.
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [ANOVA](core/anova.qmd)
Compare three or more groups, or multiple factors. One-way, factorial, ANCOVA, and repeated-measures ANOVA.
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Correlation & Regression](core/regression.qmd)
Quantify relationships and predict outcomes. Pearson correlation, simple regression, and multiple regression.
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Non-Parametric Tests](core/nonparametrics.qmd)
Rank-based alternatives for ordinal data, small samples, strong outliers, or non-normal distributions. Mann–Whitney (independent t-test), Wilcoxon signed-rank (paired t-test), Kruskal–Wallis (one-way ANOVA), and Friedman (repeated-measures ANOVA).
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Contingency Tables](core/contingency-tables.qmd)
Test associations between two categorical variables. Chi-square tests of independence, Fisher's exact, and Cramér's V.
:::
:::

---

## Advanced Topics

These analyses appear frequently in master's theses. Choose the ones relevant to your research area.

::: {.topic-grid}
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Mediation & Moderation](advanced/mediation-moderation.qmd)
Test whether an effect works *through* a third variable (mediation) or *depends on* a third variable (moderation). Uses the PROCESS module.
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Logistic Regression](advanced/logistic-regression.qmd)
Predict a binary outcome (yes/no, clinical/control) from one or more predictors. Odds ratios and classification accuracy.
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Factor Analysis](advanced/factor-analysis.qmd)
Identify underlying dimensions in a set of items. Exploratory (EFA) and Confirmatory (CFA) approaches, plus PCA.
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Power Analysis](advanced/power-analysis.qmd)
Determine how many participants you need to detect an effect of a given size. Avoid underpowered studies.
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Missing Data & Imputation](advanced/missing-data.qmd)
Understand the types of missingness (MCAR, MAR, MNAR) and when multiple imputation is appropriate.
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Meta-Analysis](advanced/meta-analysis.qmd)
Synthesise effect sizes across studies. Forest plots, heterogeneity, and moderator analysis in JASP.
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Measurement Invariance](advanced/measurement-invariance.qmd)
Test whether a scale behaves the same way across groups. Configural, metric, scalar, and strict invariance via multi-group CFA.
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Path Modeling](advanced/path-modeling.qmd)
Model systems of directed relationships among variables. Recursive vs. nonrecursive models, direct and indirect effects, full SEM.
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Latent Class / Profile Analysis](advanced/latent-class.qmd)
Identify unobserved subgroups of people from categorical (LCA) or continuous (LPA) indicators. A person-centred approach.
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Network Analysis](advanced/network-analysis.qmd)
Conceptualise constructs as networks of interacting components rather than reflections of a latent factor. GGM, regularisation, centrality.
:::
:::

---

## Reference pages

Two cross-cutting pages that pull together material referenced from every topic.

::: {.topic-grid}
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Which test should I use?](which-test.qmd)
A decision flowchart and lookup table from your research question and data to the matching test, with links into the relevant topic page.
:::
::: {.topic-card}
#### [Reporting & effect sizes](reporting.qmd)
APA-style reporting templates for every test on this site, alongside the standard effect-size conventions and the most common reporting mistakes to avoid.
:::
:::

---

## Resources

| Resource | Description |
|---|---|
| [JASP Download](https://jasp-stats.org/download/) | Free, open-source statistical software used throughout this site. |
| [Discovering Statistics Using JASP (DSUJ)](https://discoverjasp.com/) | The main textbook this site is built around (available in the UvA library). The linked companion site, *discoverjasp.com*, hosts open-access materials including datasets, worked examples, Labcoat Leni studies, and Smart Alex exercises that will be referenced throughout the paddling pool. |
| [How to Use JASP](https://jasp-stats.org/how-to-use-jasp/) | Step-by-step blog posts and gifs for specific analyses on the JASP website. |
| [JASP Video Library](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/) | The full collection of screen recordings we draw from, illustrating where to click in JASP. |
| [Scientific and Statistical Reasoning (SSR)](https://studiegids.uva.nl/en/courses/2025/1/54082) | UvA bachelor course whose lectures cover the conceptual grounding for many of the topics here. Pool pages link directly to the corresponding lecture recording where available. |

---

## Contact, feature requests & issues

Spotted a mistake, want a topic added, or have a suggestion?

Email me at [j.b.vandoorn@uva.nl](mailto:j.b.vandoorn@uva.nl), or open an issue on the [GitHub repository](https://github.com/JohnnyDoorn/paddling-pool/issues).
